name: Nihonium
name_origin: Named after Nihon, one of the Japanese names for Japan, in recognition of the RIKEN team that first synthesized it.

discovery.discoverer: RIKEN team led by Kosuke Morita

occurrence.abundance_summary: Nihonium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via reactions such as 70Zn + 209Bi (RIKEN) and as a decay product of moscovium.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of nihonium has been experimentally characterized. By analogy with its lighter group 13 homologs (indium, thallium) it is predicted to be a post-transition metal favoring the +1 oxidation state, with relativistic stabilization of the 7s2 pair making the +3 state likely less common than for thallium.
isotopes_summary: All nihonium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 286Nh with a half-life of about 10 seconds; lighter isotopes have half-lives ranging from milliseconds to seconds.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
